optInternal.option: Internal in-house multi-provider LLM abstraction (`@relavium/llm`) wrapping each provider's official TS SDK / raw HTTP behind a small unified interface — no heavy 3rd-party framework.
optInternal.summary: Build a thin, owned abstraction in `packages/llm` exposing a unified `generate()`/`stream()` over a small `LlmProvider` interface, with one adapter per provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepSeek). DeepSeek and OpenAI share one OpenAI-compatible adapter path; Anthropic and Gemini get dedicated adapters. The hard part is not the happy path (that is a weekend per provider) — it is the normalization tax (tool schemas, streaming events, stop reasons, usage fields) and the ongoing drift maintenance. For exactly four providers with a deliberately narrow surface (text + tools + streaming + usage), internal build is realistic and the BEST long-term fit for a local-first, no-lock-in, pure-TS engine. The honest risk is scope creep into provider-specific modalities (vision, caching, files, reasoning traces, parallel tool calls) where an owned layer turns into a second job. Mitigate by freezing the interface to a capability-gated lowest-common-denominator + typed escape hatch, and a per-provider conformance test suite run in CI against live APIs (nightly) and recorded fixtures (PR).
pros: Zero framework lock-in — the whole point of the no-vendor-lock-in principle. We own the interface; providers are swappable adapters, and we are never blocked waiting on a 3rd-party lib to add a model or fix a bug.

Exactly the surface we need, nothing we don't. A workflow engine needs text + tools + streaming + usage/cost + fallback. We avoid carrying a large API surface (embeddings, RAG, agents-as-a-framework) we will never call.
Runs identically in Node worker and Tauri WebView because it is just fetch + async iterators — no Node-only deps, no Vercel edge assumptions, no Python sidecar. One build target via tsup, ESM + CJS.
Cost accounting is first-class and trustworthy: our own pricing table keyed on canonical model ids, normalized usage incl. cache tokens — not dependent on a 3rd-party exposing the field we need.
Fallback chains, retry classification, and per-attempt usage are policy WE control — trivial to express per-agent (Anthropic -> OpenAI -> DeepSeek) without fighting a framework's opinions.
Debuggability: `raw` passthrough + thin adapters mean when a provider misbehaves we read OUR ~200-line adapter, not a vendor's abstraction stack. Smaller blast radius.
Tiny dependency tree improves Tauri bundle size discipline (a documented value) and supply-chain surface — only official provider SDKs, which we'd arguably pull in transitively anyway.
cons: We own the drift. Every provider API change, new model id, new param, new streaming event, new usage field is OUR ticket. Across 4 providers this is a real, recurring, non-zero tax (realistically a few hours every few weeks, spikier around major model launches).

Gemini is genuinely awkward: restricted OpenAPI-subset tool schemas (no $ref, limited formats), function-call results without ids, different streaming shape, and safety/RECITATION stop reasons. It is the adapter most likely to leak provider-specifics and the one a 3rd-party lib would have already absorbed.

We re-implement plumbing that mature libs give free: incremental tool-arg JSON assembly, SSE parsing edge cases, retry/backoff classification, partial-JSON tolerance. Each is small but each is a bug source until covered by tests.

Capability divergence forces a lowest-common-denominator interface or a typed escape hatch (`providerOptions`). Prompt caching, vision, parallel tool calls, reasoning/thinking traces, structured outputs all differ — supporting them uniformly is ongoing design work, and the escape hatch erodes the abstraction.
No community shielding: when a provider ships a breaking change, a popular lib often patches it within days for everyone; we find out via a failing conformance test (good) or a user bug report (bad).
Higher up-front cost than `npm install` + 30 lines. We pay the integration + normalization + test-suite cost before shipping the first agent, which slows time-to-first-working-CLI slightly.
optInternal.effort:
INITIAL BUILD (4 providers, streaming + tool calls + usage/cost + fallback), one experienced TS dev:
- Interface + types + pricing table + StreamChunk union + fallback runner + error taxonomy: ~2-3 days.
- OpenAI adapter (also the DeepSeek base via baseURL override): ~2-3 days incl. streaming tool-arg assembly and include_usage handling. DeepSeek then ~0.5 day (model ids, cache-hit token field, quirks).
- Anthropic adapter (typed event stream, tool_use/tool_result round-trip, system param, cache usage): ~2-3 days.
- Gemini adapter (systemInstruction, restricted tool schema reshaping + JSON-Schema sanitizer, id synthesis, safety stop reasons, stream shape): ~3-4 days — the long pole.
- Conformance test suite (one spec run against all adapters: streams text, calls a tool, returns usage, maps stop reasons, handles fallback) + recorded fixtures: ~2-3 days.
TOTAL: roughly 2.5-3.5 focused weeks to a solid, tested v1. A rough happy-path-only version is ~3-4 days but is a liability without the test suite.

ONGOING MAINTENANCE: budget a few hours every 2-4 weeks steady-state — new model ids in the pricing/capability table (frequent, cheap), param/field additions (occasional), streaming-format or schema changes (rare but expensive, mostly Gemini/Anthropic on major launches). New modalities (vision, audio, files, reasoning traces) are project-sized, not maintenance — gate them behind capabilities and only build when a workflow needs them. The conformance suite (nightly vs live APIs, fixtures on PR) converts most drift from a production incident into a red CI run, which is the single biggest leverage point.
optInternal.fitWithVision: Strongest fit of any option with the documented principles. NO-VENDOR-LOCK-IN is an explicit, adversarially-reviewed principle — an owned abstraction is the literal expression of it, and it directly resolves the user's rejection of Vercel AI SDK without substituting a different framework dependency (LangChain) that reintroduces the same lock-in concern. ENGINE-FIRST / ONE-LANGUAGE-TYPESCRIPT: `@relavium/llm` is pure TS with no Python sidecar, fits the planned packages/llm slot and the build order (shared -> llm -> core -> cli). LOCAL-FIRST PHASE 1: calls go straight from the user machine to providers using a key from the OS keychain passed into `generate()/stream()` — the adapter is stateless and keychain-agnostic, so the desktop (Tauri) reads the key via tauri-plugin-keychain and the CLI reads it from ~/.relavium, both calling the same package. RUNS IN BOTH NODE + TAURI: it is fetch + async iterators only, so the identical package executes in the Node engine/CLI worker and inside the Tauri WebView; no edge/runtime assumptions. PHASE 1 -> PHASE 2 CLEAN SWITCH: because fallback/retry/usage are our policy, moving execution to a cloud BullMQ worker reuses the same `@relavium/llm` unchanged. One concrete contradiction to flag: ADR-0004 currently names Vercel AI SDK AND the orchestration row mentions @langchain/langgraph — adopting internal build means rewriting ADR-0004 and removing the langchain reference, and the existing `packages/llm` description ('Vercel AI SDK wrappers') must change to 'in-house provider adapters'.
optInternal.lockInRisk: Lowest of all options. Lock-in is to our own ~800-1200 line abstraction, which we can refactor at will. Residual coupling is only to the official provider SDKs behind the adapter boundary (and even that is optional — adapters can use raw fetch), so swapping or dropping an SDK never touches engine/CLI/UI callers. The escape hatch (`providerOptions` + `raw`) is the one place provider-specifics leak; discipline (keep escape-hatch usage out of packages/core, confine it to opt-in agent config) keeps that contained. There is effectively zero risk of a 3rd-party abandoning the project, changing its license, or pivoting its API out from under us.
optInternal.verdict: RECOMMEND internal build as the destination, with eyes open about the Gemini adapter and the drift tax. For a deliberately narrow surface (text + tools + streaming + usage/cost + fallback) across only four providers, the normalization work is bounded and well-understood, and it is the only option that fully honors the no-lock-in principle the team already committed to. The decisive lever is the per-provider conformance suite — without it, internal build is fragile; with it, drift becomes a CI signal rather than an incident. PRAGMATIC SEQUENCING that also satisfies the user's stated fallback: because the interface above is the real asset, define `@relavium/llm`'s interface NOW and implement adapters directly against official SDKs from day one (the SDKs already do the SSE/retry plumbing, cutting the long-pole effort). This captures the no-lock-in benefit immediately without a framework, and is cheaper than people assume precisely because we lean on official SDKs inside the boundary rather than reimplementing transport. Build OpenAI+DeepSeek first (shared path, fastest to a working CLI), then Anthropic, then Gemini, gating non-core modalities behind capability flags until a workflow demands them.

optLangchain.option: LangChain.js (@langchain/core + @langchain/anthropic / @langchain/openai / @langchain/google-genai) as the multi-LLM abstraction layer
optLangchain.summary: LangChain.js gives you working, maintained chat-model classes for all 4 providers (DeepSeek via the OpenAI-compatible ChatOpenAI) with .stream(), bindTools(), and usage_metadata out of the box — so it CAN cover the LLM-call layer. But Relavium only needs the thin LLM-call slice, and that is the part where LangChain adds the least value while imposing the most cost: it drags in the Runnable/LCEL abstraction and a heavy transitive dependency graph (~100kB+ gzipped stack), leaks its own message/content-block model into your pure-TS engine, and carries a documented history of churn — it shipped v1.0 in late 2025 (createReactAgent→createAgent, deprecated APIs deleted, community/legacy chains moved to @langchain/classic, a new contentBlocks message format), and @langchain/core 0.3.x carries CVE-2026-25528 whose fix only exists in 1.x, forcing a major bump. Recent provider bugs hit exactly your needs (streaming usage double-counting cache tokens, Anthropic tool-call deltas not truly streamed). For a project whose stated principles are engine-first, one-language-TypeScript, local-first, and NO vendor lock-in, full LangChain.js is a poor fit. The only defensible variant is the narrow middle-ground: import ONLY the @langchain/<provider> chat-model classes behind your own packages/llm interface — but even that inherits @langchain/core, the Runnable base, and the churn, so it is barely cheaper than writing thin adapters over the official provider SDKs (which is the Internal-abstraction option Analyst's other track covers).
optLangchain.design:
How it maps to the 4 providers and the 5 required capabilities:

PROVIDERS
- Anthropic: `import { ChatAnthropic } from "@langchain/anthropic"` (wraps @anthropic-ai/sdk).
- OpenAI: `import { ChatOpenAI } from "@langchain/openai"` (wraps openai SDK).
- Google Gemini: `import { ChatGoogleGenerativeAI } from "@langchain/google-genai"` (wraps @google/generative-ai). (Note: Google has two integrations — google-genai vs google-vertexai/google-common — a recurring source of confusion.)
- DeepSeek: there IS a `ChatDeepSeek` in @langchain/deepseek, but it is effectively a thin ChatOpenAI subclass; you can equally point ChatOpenAI at the DeepSeek base URL. Either way it rides the OpenAI-compatible path.

CAPABILITIES
- Streaming tokens: `for await (const chunk of model.stream(messages))` yields AIMessageChunk objects; you concatenate `.content` (or read v1 `contentBlocks`). Works for all 4.
- Tool/function calling: define tools with Zod via `tool()` or schema objects, then `model.bindTools([...])`. Tool calls surface as normalized `tool_calls` on the AIMessage regardless of provider — this normalization is LangChain's single biggest real value-add (Anthropic vs OpenAI vs Gemini tool schemas genuinely differ).
- Usage + cost accounting: `response.usage_metadata` gives `{ input_tokens, output_tokens, total_tokens }` normalized across providers, plus `input_token_details` for cache/reasoning tokens. BUT cost ($) is NOT provided — you compute it from a price table yourself either way. And there is an open bug (#10249) where streaming double-counts Anthropic cache tokens, so you cannot fully trust usage_metadata for billing without your own reconciliation.
- Per-agent fallback chains: `model.withFallbacks([modelB, modelC])` exists and is genuinely convenient — but it is implemented as a Runnable, so adopting it pulls LCEL semantics into your engine. Relavium's engine should own fallback policy (it ties into cost/observability), so this is arguably better implemented in packages/core regardless of library.

RUNTIME FIT
- Node worker (CLI/engine): fine — this is LangChain.js's primary target.
- Tauri desktop WebView: the chat-model classes are designed to run in Node/edge; in a browser/WebView context you hit the usual issues (Node built-ins, larger bundle, and the fact that calling providers from the WebView with raw keys is something you'd avoid anyway — in Tauri you'd route LLM calls through the Rust side / a Node-ish worker, not the WebView). So in practice LLM calls live in the engine/worker, not the WebView, which is the same regardless of library — LangChain doesn't help here and its bundle weight hurts if it ever does load in the WebView.

MIDDLE GROUND (provider classes only): you can absolutely do `packages/llm` exposing your own LlmProvider interface, and internally instantiate ChatAnthropic/ChatOpenAI/ChatGoogleGenerativeAI, mapping their AIMessage/usage_metadata onto YOUR types so Runnables never leak past packages/llm. This is the most sensible way to use LangChain here. It still installs @langchain/core (the Runnable base, message classes, tracing hooks) under each provider package, so you do not escape the dependency weight or the version-pinning dance — but you do contain the conceptual leak.
pros: All 4 providers covered by maintained, batteries-included chat-model classes; DeepSeek rides the OpenAI-compatible path (ChatDeepSeek or ChatOpenAI+baseURL).

Cross-provider NORMALIZATION is the real value: tool_calls, message roles, and usage_metadata are unified, so your engine doesn't hand-write per-provider tool-schema and response translation. This is the genuinely tedious part of an internal build.
Unified .stream() and bindTools() APIs identical across providers — write the engine's call path once.
Built-in conveniences you'd otherwise build: withFallbacks(), withRetry(), structured-output helpers, withStructuredOutput(), and a large community knowledge base / examples.
Large ecosystem and documentation; easy to hire for and to find Stack Overflow / LLM-generated answers.
If you ever DO want graph/agent orchestration, LangGraph.js is right there — though Relavium explicitly has its own engine, so this is mostly irrelevant.
cons: You are buying a framework to use ~5% of it. Relavium has its OWN pure-TS engine; you need only the LLM-call slice — exactly where LangChain's value is thinnest (it's mostly a normalizer over the same official SDKs you could call directly) and its baggage thickest.

Concept leakage: chat models ARE Runnables. Even contained in packages/llm, the message classes (AIMessage/HumanMessage), content-block model, and the new v1 contentBlocks/outputVersion split impose LangChain's data model on your boundary. If it leaks past packages/llm, your engine becomes LangChain-shaped — direct contradiction of the 'no vendor lock-in' and 'pure-TS engine' principles.

Dependency weight: the LangChain stack is ~100kB+ gzipped vs ~34kB for a raw provider SDK, and each @langchain/<provider> ALSO bundles the underlying official SDK plus @langchain/core. You ship two layers (LangChain wrapper + vendor SDK) instead of one. Tree-shaking is imperfect because Runnable machinery and tracing hooks are pulled in.

Churn / breaking changes are documented and recent: v0.3→v1.0 (late 2025) deleted deprecated APIs, renamed createReactAgent→createAgent, moved @langchain/community and legacy chains to @langchain/classic, and introduced a new message contentBlocks format gated by outputVersion:'v1'. The 'no breaking changes until v2.0' promise is brand-new and unproven.
Security forcing-function: @langchain/core 0.3.x carries CVE-2026-25528 with the fix only in 1.x — i.e., staying on a 'stable' minor isn't an option; you're pushed onto the major-bump treadmill.

Multi-package version-pinning hell: @langchain/core is a peer dependency of every provider package; mismatched versions across @langchain/anthropic / openai / google-genai cause subtle dual-instance bugs. You must keep them lockstep.

Real, recent bugs in exactly your required features: streaming usage_metadata double-counts Anthropic cache tokens (#10249) — corrupts cost accounting; and Anthropic tool-call argument deltas aren't truly streamed (#9814) — defeats streaming-tool UX. You inherit these and wait on upstream.
Cost ($) is still your job — usage_metadata gives tokens, not money — so the 'accounting' win is partial; you maintain a price table regardless.
Pulling the framework for fallback/retry pulls LCEL semantics into orchestration that your engine should own anyway (fallback policy ties into your cost/observability layer).
optLangchain.effort:
INITIAL BUILD: Lowest of all options to first-working-call — `pnpm add @langchain/core @langchain/anthropic @langchain/openai @langchain/google-genai`, instantiate 4 classes, and .stream()/bindTools() work in a day. Writing the packages/llm wrapper that maps AIMessage+usage_metadata to your own types is maybe 2-4 days for clean containment. So ~1 week to a contained, working multi-provider layer — clearly faster than building internal adapters from scratch (which is more like 2-4 weeks for the same 4 providers with tool normalization).

ONGOING MAINTENANCE: This is where it inverts and becomes the WORST option for a small team valuing stability. You take on: (1) lockstep upgrades of 4+ @langchain packages that share a @langchain/core peer; (2) reacting to majors on LangChain's cadence, not yours (v1 just landed; CVE forced a bump); (3) absorbing upstream bugs in usage/streaming that directly hit cost and UX, with no ability to hot-patch beyond your own reconciliation layer; (4) a contentBlocks/outputVersion migration looming as v1 normalizes message format. Net: cheap to start, recurring tax forever, on someone else's schedule — the opposite of the local-first, low-dependency posture in product-constraints.md.
optLangchain.fitWithVision: POOR-TO-MIXED fit. Against the four documented principles: (1) engine-first — Relavium has its OWN pure-TS engine and explicitly is NOT using LangGraph/agent framework, so you'd adopt LangChain for the narrow LLM-call layer only, which is precisely where it's most replaceable by thin SDK adapters; (2) one-language-TypeScript — LangChain.js is TS, so no Python sidecar concern (a real win over LangChain-Python and a tie with Vercel AI SDK); (3) local-first Phase 1 — fine, LLM calls go direct from machine to provider, no cloud needed, LangChain doesn't break this; (4) NO vendor lock-in — this is the sharpest conflict: adopting LangChain's Runnable + message model in your call layer is a real lock-in vector unless rigorously contained behind packages/llm, and even then @langchain/core and its churn are baked in. Tauri specifics: LLM calls belong in the engine/worker, not the WebView (you wouldn't expose keys to WebView; keys live in OS keychain), so the desktop surface is unaffected either way — LangChain's bundle weight is a non-issue for the WebView but a mild negative for the CLI/worker binary. CLI: fine on Node. Net: it does not violate the local-first/one-language constraints, but it sits in direct tension with no-lock-in and engine-first, and it buys a framework for a job that is mostly normalization.
optLangchain.lockInRisk: MODERATE-TO-HIGH if used as a framework; LOW-MODERATE if rigidly contained to provider classes behind packages/llm. The lock-in is conceptual, not contractual: your code starts speaking AIMessage/HumanMessage/contentBlocks and Runnable semantics. The user's own stated fallback strategy ('start 3rd-party, migrate to internal later') is HARDER to execute with LangChain than with thin SDK wrappers, because LangChain's message/content model differs from the raw provider SDKs you'd migrate TO — so the migration is a re-map, not a peel-away. The mitigations (own LlmProvider interface, map types at the packages/llm boundary, never import @langchain types outside that package) are mandatory, not optional, and they reduce LangChain's value to mostly its tool-normalization. Churn risk compounds lock-in: you're locked to a dependency that breaks on its own cadence (v1 just shipped; CVE forced upgrades).
optLangchain.verdict: DO NOT adopt full LangChain.js as the framework — it conflicts with the engine-first and no-vendor-lock-in principles and taxes a small team with churn for a layer that is mostly a normalizer over the same official SDKs. Given the user rejects Vercel AI SDK on preference, the realistic shortlist becomes (A) thin INTERNAL adapters over the official provider SDKs, vs (B) the LangChain provider-classes-only middle ground. My recommendation: prefer the INTERNAL adapter, and treat LangChain.js NOT as a dependency to ship but as a free reference implementation — read how @langchain/anthropic/openai/google-genai normalize tool schemas and usage so you don't rediscover the edge cases. If, and only if, time-to-first-demo is the dominant constraint, adopt the narrow middle ground (ONLY @langchain/<provider> chat-model classes behind your own packages/llm LlmProvider interface, zero LangChain types past that boundary, no LCEL/Runnables in the engine) as an explicitly TEMPORARY bridge — which directly matches the user's stated 'start 3rd-party, migrate to internal later' fallback. But note that this middle ground is only marginally cheaper than the internal adapter (you still own a price table, cost calc, fallback policy, and usage reconciliation), while it imports @langchain/core, its CVE/upgrade treadmill, and a harder eventual migration. So the middle ground is a defensible bridge, not a destination. The cross-provider tool-call/usage normalization is the one piece worth either borrowing (copy the logic) or temporarily renting (provider-classes-only) — everything else in LangChain is overhead for Relavium's purpose-built engine.

optVercel.option: Stay on / start with a 3rd-party TS LLM library — incumbent Vercel AI SDK v4 (ADR-0004), vs Token.js, llm.js, official-SDKs-with-a-tiny-wrapper, and a LiteLLM Python sidecar. Recommendation: do NOT keep Vercel AI SDK as the documented choice (user preference is a hard input), do NOT use a LiteLLM sidecar (kills portability), and pick a thin TS library as the temporary "start-fast" layer behind the existing `packages/llm` seam.
optVercel.summary: Vercel AI SDK is genuinely the strongest engineering fit for exactly your four providers + your five requirements (streaming, tool calling, usage/cost, fallback, Node+Tauri), and it is MIT and runtime-agnostic, so the user's objection is preference-driven rather than a technical lock-in. But preference is a hard input here, and there ARE secondary non-preference concerns (single-vendor stewardship, fast-moving major versions, "ai-sdk-isms" leaking into your types) that make it reasonable to avoid stamping a Vercel-stewarded library into the ADR corpus as the canonical layer. Crucially, your architecture already isolates the provider library behind a thin `packages/llm` adapter (model id + messages + tools in; token stream + cost record out) — that seam is exactly the "start 3rd-party, migrate internal later" boundary the user wants. Among 3rd-party TS options, the best swap-out-friendly start is calling the four official provider SDKs behind your own tiny wrapper (you already need that wrapper layer; the SDKs are the part you'd keep even after going "internal"), with Token.js as the fastest zero-effort interim if you want one import today. llm.js is too thin/immature, and LiteLLM reintroduces the Python runtime ADR-0003 deliberately removed.
optVercel.design:
Your design already has the right shape. `packages/core`'s AgentRunner hands `packages/llm` (the ProviderAdapter) a model id + messages + tools and gets back a normalized token stream + cost record (see /Users/dev/Documents/Projects/Agent-Organizer/docs/architecture/multi-llm-providers.md). Every 3rd-party option below lives ENTIRELY inside `packages/llm`; the engine never imports provider types. That is the migration seam.

VERCEL AI SDK v4 (incumbent): one import gives `streamText`/`streamObject` with a unified async token+tool-call stream, provider packages `@ai-sdk/anthropic`, `@ai-sdk/openai`, `@ai-sdk/google`, and DeepSeek via its OpenAI-compatible endpoint (`@ai-sdk/openai` with a custom baseURL, or `@ai-sdk/deepseek`). Tool calling is unified (Zod-typed `tool()` defs translated to each provider's wire shape), `result.usage` gives promptTokens/completionTokens for your CostTracker, and `experimental_telemetry`/provider metadata expose reasoning + cache fields. Fallback chains you implement yourself around it (the SDK has no built-in router) — which you already planned. Pure JS, no native deps, runs in Node worker, VS Code ext host, and the Tauri WebView/sidecar Node context. This is a clean fit for all four providers and all five requirements.

OFFICIAL SDKs + TINY WRAPPER (recommended start): import `@anthropic-ai/sdk`, `openai`, `@google/generative-ai` (or `@google/genai`), and call DeepSeek through the `openai` package pointed at api.deepseek.com (it's OpenAI-compatible). Your `packages/llm` already MUST contain a ToolNormalizer + FallbackChain + CostTracker per your own architecture doc; the only extra work vs Vercel is normalizing three streaming/tool dialects yourself (Anthropic SSE `content_block_delta`, OpenAI/DeepSeek `chat.completions` deltas, Gemini `generateContentStream`). That is ~3 stream adapters, not N. The payoff: this IS the internal abstraction the user wants eventually — going "fully internal" later means deleting nothing, just not adding a unifying lib on top. Lowest lock-in of any option.

TOKEN.JS (fastest interim): single client, ~200+ models, OpenAI-style `create({stream:true})` API, bundles provider calls, supports all four of your providers, tool calling and streaming included. MIT, browser/Node. Behaves like a drop-in unified client you can put behind `packages/llm` in an afternoon, then swap for official-SDKs once you need provider-specific features. Smaller community/bus-factor than Vercel.

LLM.JS: very small unified wrapper, supports the major providers + streaming, but the tool-calling/usage normalization surface is thin and the project is single-maintainer/immature — fine for a chatbot, under-built for fallback-chains + per-node cost accounting as a product feature.

LITELLM SIDECAR (Python): the most battle-tested router (huge provider catalog, built-in fallback + budget/cost tracking + retries), but it is a Python process. It cannot load inside the VS Code extension host, bloats the 2-5MB Tauri target, and adds a process/port/lifecycle on the hot path — exactly what ADR-0003 and the current ADR-0004 already rejected. Off the table for Phase 1 local-first.
pros: Vercel AI SDK is the lowest-effort path that satisfies all five requirements for exactly your four providers today; unified streamText + Zod tool calling + usage out of the box, no Python.

It is MIT-licensed and runtime-agnostic (no Vercel account, no Vercel hosting, runs in Node/VS Code host/Tauri) — so the user's concern is brand/stewardship preference, not a technical or legal lock-in.

Official-SDKs-with-a-tiny-wrapper has the lowest lock-in of all options and doubles as the eventual internal abstraction: the SDKs are the layer you keep even after 'going internal', so migration cost is near zero.
Token.js gives a true one-import unified client covering all four providers in an afternoon — ideal as a throwaway start-fast layer behind the existing packages/llm seam.

Your architecture already isolates the provider lib behind a thin ProviderAdapter (model id + messages + tools -> token stream + cost record), so ANY of these can be swapped without touching the engine — the 'migrate internal later' path is already built in.
Avoiding a Python sidecar (LiteLLM) preserves the portability and tiny-bundle wins from ADR-0001/0003 with no new process boundary on the hot path.
cons: Vercel AI SDK is single-vendor-stewarded with a fast major-version cadence (v3->v4->v5 churn); its types and 'streamText' idioms can leak into packages/llm if the wrapper is sloppy, raising migration cost later — and it's stamped with the Vercel brand the user wants to avoid, which is a hard input.

Fallback chains, per-node/per-model cost accounting, and provider-specific escape hatches (cache control, reasoning) are NOT free in any TS library — you build them yourself regardless of choice, so the library only saves the streaming/tool-normalization slice.

Official-SDKs path means owning ~3 streaming/tool-call dialect adapters (Anthropic vs OpenAI/DeepSeek vs Gemini) and tracking each SDK's breaking changes yourself — more up-front code than a unified lib.

Token.js and especially llm.js have small communities / single-maintainer bus-factor; provider-specific newest features (e.g., latest Gemini or Anthropic cache controls) may lag, and you'd drop to a direct call as an escape hatch.
llm.js is under-built for product-grade usage/cost normalization and fallback — fine for chat, risky as the backbone of a cost-tracking feature.

LiteLLM, though the most feature-complete router, is a Python process: incompatible with the VS Code extension host, bloats the Tauri bundle, and adds a port/lifecycle/failure mode on the hot path — disqualified by ADR-0003's portability constraint.
optVercel.effort: Build: Vercel AI SDK behind packages/llm ~ 0.5-1 week to wire all four providers + Zod tools + usage mapping (fallback/cost are separate work you owe anyway). Token.js ~ a few days, similar shape. Official-SDKs + wrapper ~ 1.5-3 weeks because you write ~3 streaming/tool-normalization adapters and a unified tool-call type yourself (this is the work the unified libs save). LiteLLM sidecar ~ low to stand up the proxy but high integration tax (process supervision, ports, packaging into Tauri AND VS Code — effectively impossible in the extension host), so its real effort is 'unbounded / blocked'. Ongoing maintenance: Vercel AI SDK = track its (frequent) major versions; Token.js/llm.js = depend on small-team upkeep, occasional direct-call escape hatches; Official-SDKs = track 3-4 SDK changelogs but each is first-party, stable, and the one you'd keep long-term, so this is the most durable maintenance profile. Critically, because everything sits behind the existing ProviderAdapter seam, switching between these later is days, not a rewrite.
optVercel.verdict: Drop Vercel AI SDK as the CANONICAL documented choice (supersede ADR-0004) on the user's hard preference — it has no technical lock-in, but the user doesn't want a Vercel-stewarded lib and there's enough secondary stewardship/version-churn risk to make that reasonable. Do NOT adopt a LiteLLM Python sidecar (it violates the portability/tiny-bundle constraints ADR-0003 already settled). Among 3rd-party TS options, the best 'start fast, migrate internal later' choice is to call the four official provider SDKs behind your own tiny wrapper inside packages/llm: it has the lowest lock-in, it doubles as the internal abstraction the user ultimately wants (migration becomes a no-op), and the four providers are all official-SDK or OpenAI-compatible so coverage is complete. If the team wants to move even faster for the very first milestone, start on Token.js (one unified import, all four providers, an afternoon's work) strictly as a throwaway layer behind the same seam, and graduate to official-SDKs as provider-specific features (Anthropic cache control, Gemini/DeepSeek specifics) demand escape hatches. llm.js is too thin for a cost-tracking, fallback-chain product. Either way, the engine never imports a provider type — the swap remains a days-not-rewrite change because the packages/llm ProviderAdapter boundary already exists.
optVercel.fitWithVision: Strong fit on every axis EXCEPT the new no-Vercel directive. Pure-TS engine: all TS options (Vercel, Token.js, llm.js, official SDKs) are pure JS with no native deps, so they load identically in the Node CLI/worker, the VS Code extension host, and the Tauri WebView/sidecar Node context — preserving ADR-0003's portability. The LiteLLM sidecar is the one option that BREAKS this (Python runtime can't live in the VS Code ext host, bloats the 2-5MB Tauri bundle). Local-first: every TS option calls providers directly from the user's machine with keys read from the OS keychain at call time (ADR-0006) — no cloud, no account, matching the local-first constraint; LiteLLM would add a process even for local use. No-lock-in: official-SDKs-with-a-wrapper is the strongest no-lock-in answer and is literally the internal abstraction the principle implies; Vercel AI SDK is technically no-lock-in (MIT, runtime-agnostic) but conflicts with the user's brand preference. The git-native YAML fallback_chain feature is implemented by YOUR FallbackChain regardless of library, so it's unaffected by this choice. CLI + Tauri + VS Code all share the same packages/llm — the recommendation keeps that single shared layer intact.
optVercel.lockInRisk: Low-to-medium and fully containable. Technical lock-in for Vercel AI SDK is genuinely low: MIT license, runtime-agnostic, no Vercel account/hosting required, and the engine only touches it through the packages/llm ProviderAdapter — the engine has zero provider/SDK imports. The real lock-in risks are softer: (1) Vercel single-vendor stewardship + roadmap control + fast major-version churn (v3->v4->v5) can leak 'streamText'-style idioms and SDK types into the wrapper if discipline slips, raising migration cost — mitigated by keeping packages/llm types your own and never re-exporting SDK types; (2) Token.js/llm.js carry bus-factor risk (small/single-maintainer projects) more than lock-in risk. Official-SDKs-with-a-wrapper has the LOWEST lock-in of all options because the SDKs are first-party and are the layer you keep even when you go 'fully internal'. LiteLLM's lock-in is architectural, not licensing: adopting it locks you into shipping and supervising a Python process everywhere, which is the heaviest and least reversible commitment of the set.

optStrategy.option: Define an internal `LLMProvider` seam in @relavium/llm as the immovable contract; build hand-rolled adapters for the 4 providers behind it from day 1 (Option 1), with a 3rd-party-behind-the-seam fallback (Option 2) reserved for a named trigger.
optStrategy.summary: The user's core architectural insight is correct AND the existing docs already encode it: the engine talks to packages/llm through a provider-agnostic boundary (AgentRunner hands it modelId+messages+tools, gets back a token stream + cost record; the public RunEvent contract carries no SDK types). If that seam is defined as OUR stable interface and NOTHING SDK-shaped leaks across it, swapping the implementation later is cheap and localized. Migration only gets expensive if SDK types, streaming shapes, or tool-call representations leak past the seam. Given only 4 well-documented providers (3 of which are OpenAI-wire-compatible), "internal from day 1 behind our own seam" is the lower total-cost path AND the only one that fully honors the documented no-vendor-lock-in + one-language-TS principles. The remaining real question is hand-rolled-vs-3rd-party as the IMPLEMENTATION behind the seam — and because the seam makes that reversible, it stops being a high-stakes bet. Recommend: build the seam now (non-negotiable), implement it with thin hand-rolled adapters first; adopt a 3rd-party behind the seam only if a concrete trigger fires.
pros: VALIDATES the user's insight: with a leak-free seam, the implementation choice (hand-rolled vs 3rd-party) becomes reversible and localized to packages/llm — it stops being a one-way door, which de-risks the entire decision.
Zero sunk cost today: the repo is docs-only, so the seam can be born internal-first with no rip-out of an already-wired dependency.

Only 4 providers, effectively ~2 adapter shapes (Anthropic native + OpenAI-wire for OpenAI/DeepSeek/Gemini-compat), makes hand-rolled total cost low — you are NOT signing up to maintain 100 providers.
Strongest possible alignment with documented principles: 'no vendor lock-in' becomes structurally true (not aspirational), and 'one-language-TS / engine-first' is preserved with no extra runtime.

Removes the exact risk ADR-0004 already flagged in its Negatives (coupling to SDK provider-adapter coverage & release cadence, capabilities normalized away) by making 3rd-party optional and swappable rather than load-bearing.
Full control over the killer feature (cross-provider fallback chains) and over cost accounting precision (micro-units) — no waiting on a vendor to expose a usage field or a provider capability.
Honors the user's stated Vercel preference without a knee-jerk re-pick: the seam means we are not betting the engine on ANY single vendor SDK.
cons: Hand-rolled adapters mean WE own the long-tail breakage: per-provider streaming-format quirks, tool-call JSON-fragment reassembly, rate-limit/error-code mapping, and silent wire-format changes — the SDK would absorb some of that.
Up-front effort is higher than `pnpm add ai` + four 1-line provider registrations; the seam + 2 adapter shapes + normalizer/cost wiring is a real (if bounded) build.

Risk of UNDER-designing the seam: if LLMChunk/CanonicalTool miss a real capability (e.g. parallel tool calls, reasoning/thinking tokens, prompt caching, structured-output/JSON-mode), you pay a seam-revision cost that touches AgentRunner too — the seam must be capability-complete on day 1, not just token-complete.
A too-thin seam can normalize away provider-specific features (same critique ADR-0004 raises) unless an explicit escape-hatch/passthrough is designed in from the start.
Two failure-surface owners over time: hand-rolled means no community fixes for provider drift; you must monitor 4 providers' changelogs yourself.
optStrategy.effort:
SEAM ITSELF (the load-bearing part, required in ALL options): ~1-2 days to define LLMProvider/LLMRequest/LLMChunk/CanonicalTool as Relavium-owned types + the AgentRunner adapter→RunEvent mapping + factory. This cost is unavoidable and is paid once.

OPTION 1 (hand-rolled behind the seam): initial ~1-1.5 weeks for 2 adapter shapes (Anthropic native; OpenAI-wire shared by OpenAI/DeepSeek/Gemini-compat) including SSE/tool-fragment reassembly, usage extraction, error mapping, AbortController wiring. ONGOING: you own provider-drift watch for 4 providers — realistically a few hours per provider per significant API change, concentrated in the streaming/tool-call parsing.

OPTION 2 (3rd-party behind the seam): initial ~2-3 days (wrap one SDK's streamText into LLMChunk, map tool + usage shapes). ONGOING: you inherit the vendor's release cadence — upgrades, occasional breaking changes, waiting on the vendor to wrap a new provider feature; lower day-to-day parsing burden, higher dependency-churn burden.

KEY POINT: because the seam is identical in both, switching Option 2→Option 1 (or vice-versa) later is bounded to packages/llm internals — single-digit days, no engine/surface/contract changes. That reversibility is the whole reason the seam earns its up-front cost.
optStrategy.verdict: RECOMMEND Option 1 — internal from day 1 behind our own seam — because for 4 providers (≈2 adapter shapes) the total cost of hand-rolling is LOWER than adopt-a-3rd-party-then-rip-it-out, and it makes the documented 'no vendor lock-in' principle structurally true rather than aspirational. The user's architectural insight is CONFIRMED, with one critical caveat: the cheap-migration property holds ONLY IF no vendor type, streaming shape, or tool-call representation leaks across the seam — the engine must depend exclusively on Relavium-owned types (LLMChunk, CanonicalTool, RunEvent). The good news is the existing docs already put ToolNormalizer, FallbackChain, CostTracker, and the RunEvent union on the Relavium side of that boundary, so the architecture is already shaped for this; ADR-0004 even pre-states the leak risk in its own Negatives. DECISION CRITERIA — choose internal (Option 1) when: provider count is small and stable (true: 4), you need full control of the killer fallback + cost features (true), and lock-in must be structural (true per principles). Choose 3rd-party-behind-seam (Option 2) only if you must ship in days not weeks OR you want a temporary reference implementation while learning provider quirks. Option 3 (3rd-party DIRECTLY, no seam) is REJECTED outright — it violates no-vendor-lock-in and turns a future swap into an engine-wide rewrite; it is the only genuinely expensive-to-undo choice. MIGRATION TRIGGERS to go Option 1→2 (adopt a lib): (a) hand-rolled provider-drift maintenance exceeds ~1 day/month sustained; (b) a 5th+ provider with a novel wire format is required; (c) a must-have capability (e.g. broad provider catalog) only the lib provides. TRIGGERS to go 2→1 (drop the lib): (a) the lib blocks a needed provider feature (caching/reasoning/parallel tools) behind its release cadence; (b) a lib breaking change costs more than owning the adapter would; (c) the lib pulls in an unwanted runtime/footprint (e.g. Vercel-coupled packaging) — directly relevant to the user's stated Vercel aversion. ACTION ON ADR-0004: supersede it (per the principles doc's 'needs an ADR that supersedes, not a quiet exception' rule) with a new ADR establishing the LLMProvider seam as the decision, hand-rolled adapters as the day-1 implementation, and the above triggers as the documented migration policy — the seam, not the implementation, is the architectural commitment.
optStrategy.fitWithVision: Excellent fit, arguably better than the current ADR-0004. (1) Pure-TS engine: hand-rolled adapters are plain fetch/official-SDK calls in TypeScript — no Python, no sidecar, satisfying the 'one-language-TS' and 'no LangGraph-Python' principles as fully as the Vercel SDK does, with one fewer heavyweight dependency. (2) Tauri desktop + Node worker + VS Code host: the seam crosses none of these boundaries with platform-specific code — adapters are HTTPS + injected keychain key + AbortController, runnable identically in a Node CLI worker and the Tauri/VS Code host, preserving packages/core's documented 'zero platform-specific imports'. (3) CLI-first build order: packages/llm is built in step 1 alongside shared+core and proven by the CLI before any UI — an internal seam with thin adapters is straightforward to unit-test there, keeping the engine APIs honest. (4) Local-first: calls go directly machine→provider in Phase 1; in Phase 2 the SAME package runs in cloud workers with only the key source changing — the seam is agnostic to where the key/process lives, exactly as multi-llm-providers.md and local-first-and-security.md already specify. (5) No-vendor-lock-in: this option is the literal implementation of that principle — lock-in becomes impossible-by-construction rather than mitigated-by-discipline.
optStrategy.lockInRisk: LOW by construction — and lower than the current ADR-0004 path. The whole point of the seam is to make lock-in structural-impossible: the engine, every surface, and the Phase-2 SSE bind only to Relavium-owned types (LLMProvider, LLMChunk, CanonicalTool, RunEvent, agent.yaml's plain `provider` string), so no provider SDK or 3rd-party lib is load-bearing above packages/llm. The ONLY residual risk is a discipline risk: if a vendor type or streaming shape is allowed to leak across the seam (e.g. AgentRunner pattern-matching on an SDK's chunk object, or re-exporting a vendor enum), the cheap-swap property silently erodes — exactly the failure ADR-0004 names in its Negatives. Mitigations: (a) lint/architectural rule forbidding vendor-package imports outside packages/llm/adapters/*; (b) the seam types live in @relavium/shared and the engine imports ONLY those; (c) a conformance test suite the seam must pass, run against every adapter, so swapping implementations is provably behavior-preserving. With those guards, switching hand-rolled↔3rd-party↔different-lib is a packages/llm-internal change measured in single-digit days, with zero changes to engine, surfaces, or contracts.

llmDecision.recommendation: Build an internal, Relavium-owned multi-LLM abstraction in `packages/llm` (`@relavium/llm`): a single provider-agnostic `LLMProvider` seam expressed only in Relavium/Zod types, implemented from day one by thin hand-rolled adapters over each provider's official TS SDK (Anthropic and Gemini dedicated; OpenAI and DeepSeek share one OpenAI-compatible adapter). The seam — modelId + messages + tools in, normalized chunk stream + cost record out, with no vendor type ever crossing it — is the immovable contract; the adapter implementation behind it is deliberately reversible. Migration stance: internal-first, no 3rd-party framework; adopt a thin TS library behind the same seam only if a named trigger fires (e.g. sustained provider-drift maintenance cost or a must-have capability not economically hand-rollable), never the Vercel AI SDK.
llmDecision.rationale: All four analyses converge on the seam, and three of four (A, B, D) recommend an internal implementation behind it; Analyst C, who rates Vercel strongest on pure engineering, explicitly concedes the user's no-Vercel preference is a hard input and that the best swap-friendly start is thin wrappers over the official provider SDKs — which is the internal option. So the recommendation honors the user's hard constraint (no Vercel) and their stated lean (internal, with a 3rd-party bridge as fallback) while still being defensible on the merits. It is the only option that fully satisfies the documented principles: one-language-TypeScript and engine-first (no Python sidecar, no LiteLLM), and crucially NO vendor lock-in — Analyst B shows full LangChain.js violates this (Runnable/LCEL leakage, ~100kB+ transitive graph, v1.0 churn, CVE-2026-25528 in 0.3.x forcing a major bump, provider bugs hitting our exact needs), and even the narrow @langchain/<provider> variant inherits @langchain/core and is barely cheaper than hand-rolled adapters. The scope is genuinely small and bounded (four well-documented providers, three OpenAI-wire-compatible) and the surface is deliberately narrow (text + tools + streaming + usage), so Analyst A's internal build is realistic; the only real risk — normalization tax and provider drift into vision/caching/reasoning modalities — is mitigated by freezing a capability-gated lowest-common-denominator interface with a typed providerOptions escape hatch and a per-provider conformance test suite (recorded fixtures on PR, live nightly). Analyst D's key point makes the bet low-stakes: because nothing SDK-shaped crosses the seam, swapping the implementation later is cheap and localized, so "internal now, 3rd-party-behind-seam only on a named trigger" is the lowest-total-cost, fully-reversible path.
llmDecision.revisedAdr0004:
# ADR-0004: Internal multi-LLM abstraction (`@relavium/llm`), not a 3rd-party framework

- **Status**: Accepted (supersedes the earlier *Vercel AI SDK for multi-LLM* decision recorded under this number)
- **Date**: 2026-06-03
- **Related**: [0003-pure-ts-engine-not-langgraph-python.md](0003-pure-ts-engine-not-langgraph-python.md), [0006-os-keychain-for-api-keys.md](0006-os-keychain-for-api-keys.md), [tech-stack.md](../tech-stack.md)

## Context

This ADR replaces the original ADR-0004, which selected the **Vercel AI SDK** as the multi-LLM layer. That choice is withdrawn: the project will not adopt Vercel-stewarded products. The objection is a hard product input, not a defect claim — the Vercel AI SDK is MIT and runtime-agnostic — but it removes it from consideration, and the alternatives were re-evaluated from scratch.

Relavium is multi-model by definition: an agent node names a model from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google (Gemini), or DeepSeek, and the engine must call any of them through one code path. Two hard requirements fall out of that, plus three more from product scope:

- **Unified streaming.** Every provider streams tokens and tool calls differently. The engine emits a single normalized [run-event stream](../reference/contracts/sse-event-schema.md) (`node.token`, `agent:tool_call`, …) regardless of which provider produced it.
- **Cross-provider tool normalization.** Tool/function-calling schemas differ per provider; the engine presents one canonical tool model and translates to each wire format.
- **Usage + cost accounting**, **per-agent fallback chains**, and execution on **both a Node worker (CLI/engine) and inside a Tauri desktop context**.

This layer lives in `packages/llm` and is consumed by the pure-TypeScript engine from [ADR-0003](0003-pure-ts-engine-not-langgraph-python.md). It must run in every host the engine runs in (desktop, CLI, VS Code extension, Phase-2 cloud workers), so it cannot pull in a separate runtime. API keys are read from the OS keychain per [ADR-0006](0006-os-keychain-for-api-keys.md) and never exposed to the frontend. The target provider set is small (four) and well documented, and three of the four (OpenAI, DeepSeek, Gemini) are reachable over an OpenAI-compatible wire format.

## Decision

**We build an internal, Relavium-owned multi-LLM abstraction in `packages/llm` (`@relavium/llm`).** Its centre is a single provider-agnostic seam — an `LLMProvider` interface expressed only in Relavium/Zod types — implemented by thin hand-rolled adapters over each provider's official TypeScript SDK. We do **not** adopt the Vercel AI SDK, LangChain.js, or any other multi-LLM framework, and we do **not** run a Python sidecar (LiteLLM).

The seam is the immovable contract; the adapter implementation behind it is deliberately reversible. `AgentRunner` (in `packages/core`) hands `packages/llm` a model id, a message list, and tools, and gets back a normalized chunk stream (`text` / `tool_call` / `usage` / `finish`) plus a cost record. **No vendor SDK type ever crosses that seam.** Anthropic and Gemini get dedicated adapters; OpenAI and DeepSeek share one OpenAI-compatible adapter.

Considered options:

1. **Internal abstraction over official provider SDKs (`@relavium/llm`)** — owned seam, thin per-provider adapters, no framework. *Chosen.*
2. **Vercel AI SDK** — the original choice; withdrawn on the no-Vercel-products constraint (a hard input).
3. **LangChain.js (`@langchain/core` + per-provider chat models)** — working multi-provider classes, but it drags in the Runnable/LCEL abstraction and a heavy transitive graph, leaks its own message/content-block model into the engine, and carries documented churn (v1.0 reshape, `@langchain/core` 0.3.x exposed to CVE-2026-25528 whose fix lands only in 1.x). Even the narrow "import only the `@langchain/<provider>` chat models behind our seam" variant inherits `@langchain/core` and is barely cheaper than hand-rolled adapters.
4. **LiteLLM Python sidecar** — mature router, but reintroduces the Python runtime [ADR-0003](0003-pure-ts-engine-not-langgraph-python.md) deliberately removed; cannot live in the VS Code extension host and bloats the Tauri bundle ([ADR-0001](0001-tauri-v2-over-electron.md)).

The internal abstraction wins because it is the only option that fully honors Relavium's documented principles — engine-first, one-language-TypeScript, local-first, and **no vendor lock-in** — for a deliberately narrow surface (text + tools + streaming + usage) against just four providers. The happy path per provider is small; the real cost is the *normalization tax* (tool schemas, streaming events, stop reasons, usage fields) and ongoing provider drift. That cost is bounded and is exactly what we would pay to a framework in coupling and churn instead. We contain it by **freezing the interface to a capability-gated lowest-common-denominator plus a typed `providerOptions` escape hatch**, and by a **per-provider conformance test suite** — recorded fixtures on PR, live APIs nightly in CI.

Because nothing SDK-shaped crosses the seam, replacing the implementation later is cheap and localized. We therefore adopt an **internal-first, framework-never (esp. Vercel-never) stance with a reversible 3rd-party bridge**: a thin TS library *behind the same seam* (e.g. Token.js) becomes an option only if a **named trigger** fires — sustained provider-drift maintenance cost exceeding the framework's coupling cost, or a must-have capability that is not economically hand-rollable. The trigger fires a follow-up ADR; it never silently changes the seam.

Provider details and built-in tool wiring are in [architecture/multi-llm-providers.md](../architecture/multi-llm-providers.md) and [reference/shared-core/built-in-tools.md](../reference/shared-core/built-in-tools.md). Pinned SDK versions live in [tech-stack.md](../tech-stack.md).

## Consequences

### Positive

- One TypeScript code path for every provider, with zero framework dependency; the engine emits a single normalized [run-event stream](../reference/contracts/sse-event-schema.md) regardless of which model ran.
- **No vendor lock-in and no leaked vendor types.** The seam is pure Relavium/Zod; the only third-party code is each provider's own official SDK, which we would keep even after any future migration.
- No Python runtime and no sidecar — `packages/llm` loads in the same hosts as the engine (desktop, CLI, VS Code extension, Phase-2 cloud workers), preserving the portability won in [ADR-0003](0003-pure-ts-engine-not-langgraph-python.md).
- Provider-agnostic tool calling: agents declare tools once; the `ToolNormalizer` translates to each wire format, which is what makes cross-provider fallback chains possible.
- Adding a provider is a contained adapter change in one package; OpenAI and DeepSeek already share one adapter path.
- The migration story is real, not aspirational: because the seam admits no vendor type, a 3rd-party bridge can be slotted behind it on a named trigger without touching `packages/core`.
- API keys stay engine-side: read from the OS keychain ([ADR-0006](0006-os-keychain-for-api-keys.md)) and attached by the adapter, never reaching the frontend.

### Negative

- We own the normalization tax and ongoing provider drift (tool schemas, streaming events, stop reasons, usage fields) that a framework would otherwise absorb. Mitigated by a frozen lowest-common-denominator interface, a typed `providerOptions` escape hatch, and a conformance suite (recorded fixtures on PR, live nightly in CI).
- Provider-specific modalities (vision, prompt caching, files, reasoning traces, parallel tool calls) are out of the common interface by default; surfaced only through `providerOptions` to prevent scope creep into a "second product".
- More code to write and test up front than importing a ready-made multi-provider library.
- We forgo a framework's broader long-tail provider catalog; acceptable because MVP scope is API-based providers only (no Ollama / local models), so the target set is small and well covered.
- Cost/token accounting depends on mapping each provider's usage fields correctly into our cost model; this is an adapter responsibility, covered by the conformance suite and documented in [architecture/multi-llm-providers.md](../architecture/multi-llm-providers.md).

See [ADR-0004](decisions/0004-internal-llm-abstraction.md). |

Mandates that NO vendor SDK type — message shapes, content blocks, streaming events, tool-call representations — may cross the seam, since that leak is the only thing that makes a future implementation swap expensive. Defines the named triggers under which a 3rd-party library may be adopted behind the seam (sustained drift cost; an uneconomical-to-hand-roll capability) and requires a follow-up ADR when one fires. Note: ADR-0004 already carries the seam-and-trigger reasoning, so this companion is optional — promote it only if the seam needs to be cited independently as a standalone contract from reference/ and standards/ docs.

revArch.overallVerdict: The architecture set is high quality, internally coherent, and well cross-linked — the "one engine, thin surfaces, local-first, Phase-2-marked" model is consistent across all nine files and the Relavium rename is complete (zero "Flowgent"/"agentorg" leakage in architecture/). The single dominant problem is that the multi-LLM decision has been replaced: every file that mentions the LLM layer still presents the Vercel AI SDK as the chosen, settled foundation. multi-llm-providers.md must be substantially rewritten; overview.md, README.md, and (cross-cuttingly) ADR-0004 + tech-stack.md + architectural-principles.md all assert the now-rejected choice. Separately and independent of the LLM pivot, there is a real consistency break: all flow docs use the LEGACY dotted run-event names (node.token, run.complete, cost.update, seqNo) while citing the SSE schema as canonical, but that schema has moved to colon-namespaced names (agent:token, run:completed, sequenceNumber) and explicitly labels the dotted form legacy. There are also smaller drift items (fallback_chain field name attempts vs max_attempts, gemini model-id format, micro-units vs micro-cents). Fix order: (1) rewrite multi-llm-providers.md to the internal @relavium/llm abstraction and re-point the ADR link; (2) scrub Vercel mentions in overview.md/README.md; (3) reconcile the run-event names across all four flow docs; (4) the cross-cutting ADR-0004 supersession is a decisions/ task but must be flagged because every architecture doc links to it as "Accepted".
crossCutting: ADR-0004 (decisions/0004-vercel-ai-sdk-multi-llm.md) is still Status: Accepted and is the authoritative target of every multi-LLM cross-link in architecture/. Per the new decision it must be Superseded by a new ADR (e.g. 0011) that records the internal @relavium/llm abstraction, the LLMProvider seam (Relavium/Zod types only, no vendor type crossing it), the adapter strategy (Anthropic + Gemini dedicated, OpenAI + DeepSeek sharing one OpenAI-compatible adapter), and the internal-first migration stance with named triggers and explicit 'never the Vercel AI SDK' rule. Until that exists, the architecture docs cannot link to a correct ADR. Every architecture file that says '[ADR-0004] — the Vercel AI SDK decision' needs its link/label updated once 0011 lands.

tech-stack.md row 'Multi-LLM | Vercel AI SDK v4' and the 'Notes on the Choices' bullet ('The Vercel AI SDK gives one unified streaming interface') are the version-of-record both the architecture docs and ADRs defer to; they assert the rejected choice and must be updated to '@relavium/llm — internal abstraction over official provider SDKs'. This is outside architecture/ but every architecture doc inherits its framing from here.

standards/architectural-principles.md §2 ('One language — TypeScript') states 'multi-LLM streaming goes through the Vercel AI SDK' — a binding principle that now contradicts the decision; must be reworded to 'multi-LLM streaming goes through Relavium's own packages/llm abstraction (thin adapters over each provider's official SDK)'. The new decision also arguably deserves a new principle: 'No vendor lock-in at the LLM seam — the LLMProvider contract is expressed only in Relavium/Zod types; no vendor type crosses it.'

Run-event naming is inconsistent system-wide: the canonical reference/contracts/sse-event-schema.md now uses colon-namespaced names (agent:token, node:completed, run:completed, human_gate:paused/resumed) with a `sequenceNumber` field, and explicitly tags the dotted names (node.token, run.complete, cost.update, seqNo) as LEGACY. But execution-model.md, state-management.md, overview.md, and shared-core-engine.md all still use the dotted/legacy names and `seqNo`. Every architecture doc that names events must be reconciled to the colon form (or the schema must declare the dotted form canonical — pick one). This predates and is independent of the LLM pivot.

project-structure.md and architecture-decisions memory describe packages/llm adapters as 'Vercel AI SDK wrappers' / list AnthropicAdapter, OpenAIAdapter, GeminiAdapter, DeepSeekAdapter. The adapter inventory is actually now: AnthropicAdapter (official SDK), GeminiAdapter (official SDK), and ONE shared OpenAI-compatible adapter serving both OpenAI and DeepSeek. project-structure.md's package description ('Vercel AI SDK wrappers' is in the memory; project-structure.md itself just lists four adapters) should reflect the 3-adapter / OpenAI-compatible-shared shape and drop any Vercel wrapper framing.

revDec.overallVerdict: The decisions/ corpus is high-quality, internally consistent in tone, and faithfully follows the condensed-MADR house style (H1 + bold Status/Date/Related, Context/Decision/Consequences with Positive/Negative, cross-links to siblings and tech-stack.md, specs cited not restated). Numbering is clean (0001-0010, zero-padded), dates are uniform ISO 2026-06-03, and the README routing table (two ADR spaces, mermaid decision tree) is accurate and well-conceived. The ONE structural problem is the multi-LLM decision: ADR-0004 (Vercel AI SDK) is now contradicted by the new directive to build an internal @relavium/llm abstraction with hand-rolled per-provider adapters. ADR-0004 must NOT be rewritten (MADR rule: preserve history) — it must be marked Superseded and a new ADR-0011 (LLM abstraction seam) added. Notably, project-structure.md and the glossary ALREADY describe the per-provider-adapter shape, so the docs are internally split: ADR-0004/tech-stack/architecture say Vercel AI SDK, while project-structure/glossary describe Relavium-owned adapters. Secondary findings: an unused **Deciders** field in the template that no ADR uses, a Proposed-vs-Accepted contradiction between the template and the README, a status-enum mismatch across three files, and several genuinely missing ADRs (LLM abstraction, monorepo/tooling, error-handling/observability, testing strategy, MCP). Specific per-file findings below.

issues.issue: Core conflict with the new directive. The whole ADR decides 'We use the Vercel AI SDK as the multi-LLM provider layer', which is exactly the choice the user has now rejected on preference grounds (no Vercel products) in favor of a Relavium-owned internal abstraction with hand-rolled per-provider adapters. The ADR is now factually wrong about the project's direction.
issues.fix: Do NOT rewrite the body (MADR rule, echoed in this file's own template and README: superseded ADRs are preserved verbatim). Change only the Status line to 'Superseded by [ADR-0011](0011-internal-llm-abstraction-over-vendor-sdks.md)' (next free number), keep the Date, and add a one-line forward pointer. Create ADR-0011 carrying the new decision and full rationale.

issues.issue: The new chosen approach is literally listed here as rejected option #3: 'Per-provider official SDKs, normalized by hand — maximum control, maximum boilerplate', dismissed because 'Hand-rolling per-provider SDKs gives the most control but reimplements precisely the streaming/tool-normalization work the SDK already provides.' The superseding ADR-0011 must directly rebut this exact sentence so the reversal is reasoned, not silent.
issues.fix: In ADR-0011's Decision, explicitly address why the 'maximum boilerplate' objection is now accepted: the seam (modelId + messages + tools in -> normalized chunk stream + cost record out, no vendor type crossing it) is the immovable contract; adapters are thin and deliberately reversible; Anthropic + Gemini get dedicated adapters, OpenAI + DeepSeek share one OpenAI-compatible adapter; and name the migration trigger (sustained provider-drift cost or a must-have non-hand-rollable capability) that would justify adopting a thin TS lib behind the same seam — never the Vercel AI SDK.

issues.issue: Negative-consequence mitigation already anticipates the reversal ('Mitigated by keeping packages/llm a thin, swappable wrapper rather than leaking SDK types across the engine'). This is the seam the new direction formalizes — good provenance, but it reads as self-undermining once superseded.
issues.fix: Leave as-is in the superseded body; reference it in ADR-0011 as evidence the swap was always architecturally cheap (the seam was a stated invariant from day one).

gaps: No version of this ADR ever pinned 'v4' — the body says 'Vercel AI SDK' generically while tech-stack.md and architecture/multi-llm-providers.md say 'v4'. Minor, but the version discipline (versions only in tech-stack.md) is correctly followed by the ADR itself.

Once ADR-0004 is superseded, this mitigation points at a rejected decision and the rationale weakens (internal hand-rolled adapters do NOT borrow ecosystem maturity the way a popular SDK would).
issues.fix: This ADR stays Accepted (the pure-TS engine decision is untouched), so per MADR do not rewrite history. Instead, note in ADR-0011 that it inherits and re-answers this concern: the mitigation shifts from 'a mature SDK' to 'a deliberately thin seam plus the official per-provider SDKs (which are themselves mature).' revRef.overallVerdict: Strong, coherent reference quadrant — well cross-linked, genuinely DRY, and consistent on the immovable contracts (RunEvent union, IPC primitives, config resolution, YAML specs, secrets-never-cross-the-boundary). The single biggest issue created by the new multi-LLM decision is structural, not textual: the reference docs contain NO literal "Vercel"/"AI SDK" strings (good), but they DELEGATE the entire LLM/provider contract to architecture/multi-llm-providers.md and ADR-0004, both of which are Vercel-saturated and now wrong. Because of that delegation there is currently NO canonical home in the reference quadrant for the new Relavium-owned LLMProvider seam (modelId + messages + tools in -> normalized chunk stream + cost record out, no vendor type crossing). This is both a correctness gap (cross-links now point at stale content) and a one-canonical-home gap (the immovable seam has no DRY home). Recommended: add reference/shared-core/llm-provider-seam.md as the canonical home for the seam + normalized chunk/usage/cost types, repoint agent-yaml-spec and shared-core/README at it, and have architecture/multi-llm-providers.md + ADR-0004 be rewritten/superseded to cite it. Secondary issues: dated/inconsistent model ids (gpt-4o, gemini id format), SSE schema lacks any cost event though cost streaming is promised elsewhere, node-types "nine" miscount and parallel/merge engine-enum collision, and a model_catalog FK design that fights the provider-agnostic seam and cross-provider fallback. None of these block the docs; they are accuracy/consistency fixes plus one new canonical file.
crossCutting: NEW LLM DECISION — the immovable seam has no canonical home in reference/. The reference quadrant's own README (line 21) says shared-core covers 'packages/core AND packages/llm', yet there is NO reference/shared-core file documenting @relavium/llm. The LLMProvider seam (modelId + messages + tools in; normalized chunk stream + cost record out; no vendor type ever crossing) is the new immovable contract — by the reference quadrant's own charter ('single source of truth for every Relavium contract, schema, and shape') it MUST have a canonical home here. Today every link about the LLM layer (agent-yaml-spec.md lines 6/47/78, shared-core/README.md line 3 & footer) points OUT to architecture/multi-llm-providers.md, which is entirely built on 'Vercel AI SDK v4' (its mermaid diagram, Context section, streamText/streamObject references, ADR-0004 citation). FIX: create reference/shared-core/llm-provider-seam.md as the canonical home for the seam interface, the normalized ProviderChunk/StreamEvent shape, the UsageRecord/CostRecord shape, the adapter map (Anthropic dedicated, Gemini dedicated, OpenAI+DeepSeek shared OpenAI-compatible adapter), and the fallback-chain contract; then repoint the reference cross-links at it and demote architecture/multi-llm-providers.md to narrative that cites it.

VERCEL/LANGCHAIN COUPLING IS UPSTREAM OF reference/, NOT INSIDE IT. Confirmed via grep: zero 'Vercel'/'ai-sdk' strings in reference/. The decision-flagging the orchestrator asked for therefore lands on: architecture/multi-llm-providers.md (Vercel throughout), decisions/0004-vercel-ai-sdk-multi-llm.md (the ADR itself — must be superseded, NOT by a Vercel choice), tech-stack.md line 18 & 30-32 (Multi-LLM = Vercel AI SDK v4; LiteLLM dismissed), and the architecture-decisions MEMORY file lines 16/17/34 (Vercel AI SDK v4; '@langchain/langgraph'; packages/llm = 'Vercel AI SDK wrappers'). These are outside the reviewed directory but every LLM cross-link in reference/ resolves into them, so they must be updated in lockstep or the reference docs become misleading. packages/llm description in the memory ('Provider adapters (Vercel AI SDK wrappers)') directly contradicts the new 'hand-rolled adapters over official SDKs' direction.

MODEL-ID HYGIENE for a mid-2026 product. Across reference/ the only non-Anthropic concrete model ids are 'gpt-4o' (agent-yaml-spec, workflow-yaml-spec) and 'gemini-2.5-pro'. 'gpt-4o' is a 2024-era id and reads stale in examples; pick a current/placeholder convention and apply consistently. Also a cross-doc format drift: reference uses 'gemini-2.5-pro' while architecture/multi-llm-providers.md line 93 uses 'gemini-2-5-pro' (dashes vs dots) — agree on ONE canonical model-id grammar since modelId is the seam's primary key and the LSP/completion + cost-catalog lookups depend on exact-match ids.

DEEPSEEK UNDER-SPECIFIED relative to its first-class status. The decision names DeepSeek a day-one provider (sharing the OpenAI-compatible adapter), and agent-yaml-spec lists 'deepseek' as a valid provider, but NO reference doc shows a DeepSeek example, model id, base_url, or notes that it routes through the OpenAI-compatible adapter. The new llm-provider-seam.md should state the adapter map explicitly (anthropic->dedicated, google->dedicated, openai+deepseek->shared OpenAI-compatible) so the 'provider' field's relationship to the adapter is canonical rather than implied.

FALLBACK-CHAIN SHAPE DRIFT across three docs. agent-yaml-spec uses fallback_chain entries of { model, provider, max_attempts }; the architecture doc uses { model, attempts } (no provider, different key name); the database-schema stores fallback_chain inside agents.config JSON with no shape note. Since fallback is one of the five seam-required capabilities and a headline feature, lock ONE shape (the reference's { model, provider, max_attempts }) and have the architecture doc cite it instead of restating a divergent form.

COST-EVENT GAP IN THE CANONICAL EVENT UNION. sse-event-schema.md's RunEvent union has NO cost/usage event type, yet: its own intro promises 'cost waterfalls' as a live-rendered concern; runStore (store-shapes) has a costAccumulator fed 'directly by the RunEvent stream'; tauri-plugins and ipc-contract say channels stream 'cost updates'; and architecture/multi-llm-providers emits 'cost.update events'. Today cost only rides inside node:completed.tokensUsed (no USD, no per-token-chunk cost). Decide explicitly: either (a) cost is derived client-side from tokensUsed + model_catalog pricing (then say so and drop 'cost update' as a stream event everywhere), or (b) add a first-class cost/usage event to the union. The seam's required 'usage+cost accounting' output makes this a contract-level decision, not cosmetic.

DESKTOP-IS-A-PROXY CONTRADICTION for LLM calls. ipc-contract.md line 75 states the Rust backend makes LLM streaming calls ('As LLM tokens arrive from the streaming HTTP response (reqwest with the stream feature)'), and keychain-and-secrets says the Rust backend attaches the Authorization header. But the engine + @relavium/llm is pure TypeScript and (per the new decision) must run inside a Tauri desktop context AND a Node worker via the same seam. If @relavium/llm runs in the WebView/JS side, the streaming HTTP call is NOT reqwest-in-Rust; if it runs in Rust, then @relavium/llm is not the thing making the call. Clarify where the seam executes on desktop (JS engine in WebView calling fetch, vs Rust calling reqwest) because keychain access, AbortController cancellation, and cost accounting all hinge on it. This is the 'runs on BOTH a Node worker and inside a Tauri desktop context' requirement and the docs are currently inconsistent about it.

issues.issue: agents.model_id is NOT NULL REFERENCES model_catalog(id), and step_executions.model_id / run_costs.model_id similarly FK to model_catalog(id) (lines 125, 217, 298). The new seam treats modelId as a provider-scoped string and supports fallback to models that may not be pre-seeded in the catalog; a strict FK will reject runs that use a model not yet in model_catalog (e.g. a brand-new id or a fallback target).
issues.fix: Either (a) relax to a soft reference (store the raw provider model-id string + nullable catalog FK), or (b) define an upsert/auto-seed path so any model the seam can address is guaranteed a catalog row. Document how fallback-chain target models get cost-priced when absent from the catalog.
