revStd.overallVerdict: The four Relavium standards files (README, documentation-style, adr-template, architectural-principles) are well-written, internally cross-linked, and faithful to the documented house style — documentation-style.md in particular is mature and binding-grade. BUT the standards set is materially INCOMPLETE relative to the user's own house standard (OS-Project ships 14 standards; Relavium has 4). The four present standards cover only docs/ADR concerns; there is NO binding rule governing source code, commits, reviews, testing, security, error handling, or logging — even though architectural-principles.md repeatedly promises code rules ("every surface", "full unit test coverage", "clean execution interface") and the engine-first build order assumes a tested engine. Two correctness defects need immediate fixes: (1) architectural-principles.md §2 is now WRONG — it codifies "multi-LLM streaming goes through the Vercel AI SDK" as a binding principle, which the new internal-@relavium/llm-abstraction decision reverses; (2) adr-template.md ("start with Status: Proposed") and decisions/README.md ("Start at status Accepted once the decision is settled") give contradictory ADR-status workflow guidance. architectural-principles.md is also self-labeled "draft — to be expanded" with a deferred "To be expanded" list, so it is not yet complete as a binding document. Prioritized gap: add code-style-typescript, then testing, error-handling, code-review, commit-style, security-review, logging-and-observability (mirroring the OS-Project set, retargeted from Rust/no_std to the TS monorepo + Tauri + local-first key-handling context).

issues.issue: Principle 2 (One language — TypeScript) hard-codes the now-reversed Vercel AI SDK choice as a binding rule: 'multi-LLM streaming goes through the Vercel AI SDK, and orchestration is implemented directly in TypeScript.' The new direction is an internal, Relavium-owned abstraction in packages/llm (@relavium/llm) with hand-rolled thin adapters over each provider's official SDK behind a vendor-type-free LLMProvider seam; the Vercel AI SDK is explicitly rejected. A binding principle now asserts something false.
issues.fix: Rewrite the second half of §2 to drop the Vercel AI SDK clause. State that multi-LLM goes through the internal @relavium/llm abstraction (a provider-agnostic LLMProvider seam expressed only in Relavium/Zod types, with thin per-provider adapters), and that no vendor LLM type crosses the seam. Cross-link the new/updated ADR that supersedes ADR-0004 and architecture/multi-llm-providers.md.

issues.issue: The document is self-labeled 'Status: draft — to be expanded' and ends with a 'To be expanded' section listing four principles not yet written (clean execution-mode interface, checkpoint-based resume, cross-provider tool normalization boundary, Zustand direct subscriptions). A binding standard that is admittedly a draft and lists missing rules is incomplete as the enforceable invariant set.
issues.fix: Flesh out the four deferred principles (or convert them to numbered principles with links to their canonical homes once those architecture docs land) and drop the 'draft' status blockquote when the set is complete. At minimum promote 'cross-provider tool normalization as the boundary between packages/llm and packages/core' into a real numbered principle now, since it is the seam the multi-LLM decision turns on.

issues.issue: The 'cross-provider tool normalization' deferred bullet still frames packages/llm as a normalization layer without naming the no-vendor-types-cross-the-seam contract that is now the immovable part of the architecture. The binding invariant (vendor SDK types never leak past the LLMProvider seam; adapter implementation is reversible) is not captured anywhere as a principle.
issues.fix: Add an explicit principle: 'Provider-agnostic LLM seam — packages/llm exposes one LLMProvider contract in Relavium/Zod types only; no provider SDK type ever crosses into packages/core. The seam is fixed; the adapter behind it is reversible.' This is the engineering-side rule the multi-LLM ADR depends on.

issues.issue: §5 Links lists 'Vercel AI SDK' as a sample external-URL term ('External URLs (Tauri, Vercel AI SDK, provider docs) may be absolute and inline.'). With the Vercel AI SDK rejected, this example now points readers at a dependency the project no longer uses.
issues.fix: Replace the Vercel AI SDK example with a neutral one, e.g. 'External URLs (Tauri, provider SDK docs, npm packages) may be absolute and inline.'

issues.issue: §7 documents the ADR status enum and required structure but does not state whether a new ADR starts at Proposed or Accepted — leaving the Proposed-vs-Accepted contradiction between adr-template.md and decisions/README.md unresolved at the place that is supposed to be the canonical style rule.
issues.fix: Add one sentence to §7 fixing the canonical workflow (e.g. 'New ADRs start at Proposed and flip to Accepted in a separate commit once settled' OR 'start Accepted when settled') and make adr-template.md and decisions/README.md both match it.

gaps: documentation-style.md is the only style guide and it covers Markdown only. There is no companion code-style standard, so the 'born-compliant' discipline it establishes for docs has no code analog despite architectural-principles §1 assuming a fully tested TS engine.

issues.issue: The template instructs 'fill it in, and start with Status: Proposed. Flip to Accepted in a separate commit once the decision is settled.' decisions/README.md §'Creating a new ADR' instead says 'Start at status Accepted once the decision is settled.' The two binding sources give contradictory ADR-status workflow guidance. (Compounding this, all 10 existing ADRs are already 'Accepted' with no 'Proposed' history, which matches the README, not the template.)
issues.fix: Pick one workflow and make adr-template.md, decisions/README.md, and documentation-style.md §7 agree. Given the existing corpus is born-Accepted, either (a) change the template comment to allow starting Accepted, or (b) keep Proposed-first and update the README to match — but they must be reconciled.

gaps: The template hard-codes '- **Deciders**: @cemililik' as the only decider; fine for solo phase but worth a note that this becomes a real list once contributors join (mirrors OS-Project's solo-phase carve-outs).

issues.issue: The 'Documents' table lists only the 3 sibling files plus itself and presents the set as complete ('This folder holds the binding rules ... and the engineering principles that govern how the platform is built'). It claims to govern 'how things must be written and built' but binds nothing about source code, commits, tests, reviews, security, errors, or logging — a scope the README itself advertises ('built here').
issues.fix: Either narrow the README's stated scope to docs+principles, or (preferred, to match house style) expand the index to include the missing code/process standards once created, with a Status column like OS-Project's standards/README.md.

issues.issue: Unlike OS-Project's standards/README.md, the index has no Status column and no 'how to propose a change to a standard' / 'reading order' sections. For a binding-rules folder the change-control process (which ADR motivates a standard; how to supersede it) is itself part of the standard's authority.
issues.fix: Add a 'Proposing a change to a standard' subsection (ADR-first, like OS-Project) and optionally a reading-order list, so standards have a defined amendment path.

crossCutting: MISSING STANDARDS (prioritized list of files Relavium should add to docs/standards/, modeled on the OS-Project house set but retargeted from Rust/no_std/kernel to the TypeScript monorepo + Tauri v2 + local-first context). P0 — code-style-typescript.md: tsconfig strictness, ESLint+Prettier as source of truth (CI rejects unformatted), naming, file/module layout for the packages/* + apps/* monorepo, no-`any`/explicit-return-type discipline, Zod-at-boundaries, the rule that no provider SDK type crosses the @relavium/llm seam, public-API doc-comment expectations. This is the single biggest gap and the one architectural-principles §1 (engine-first, fully tested) most depends on.

P0 — testing.md: test layers for a TS engine (unit on packages/core + packages/llm; contract tests that the LLMProvider seam normalizes every adapter identically; CLI integration as the 'fastest feedback loop' from principle §1; what must have tests — every node type, every adapter, every error path, every bug fix gets a regression test; coverage stance; vitest/whatever runner pinned in tech-stack; CI gates). Engine-first build order is unenforceable without this.

P1 — error-handling.md: Result/typed-error vs throw policy across engine and adapters; how provider/network/rate-limit/auth failures map into normalized run-events and the per-agent fallback chain; cost-accounting on partial/failed streams; no-leak-of-vendor-error-types past the seam; user-facing vs internal error surfaces (desktop, CLI, VS Code).

P1 — security-review.md + an error/secrets dimension: Relavium's crown-jewel is API keys in the OS keychain (ADR-0006, principle §6). Need a binding security-review trigger list (anything touching keychain/secret flow, IPC contract, the frontend boundary keys must never cross, MCP tool execution, file-system tools, workflow YAML that can execute code) and a checklist (secrets never to disk/frontend/logs, IPC input validation, redaction). OS-Project's security-review.md is the template; retarget from capabilities/kernel to keychain/IPC/tool-execution.

P1 — commit-style.md: Conventional Commits + scopes matching the monorepo (core, llm, shared, cli, desktop, vscode, api, portal, ui, docs, standards, adr, deps), trailers (Refs: ADR-NNNN), granularity, AI-authored-commit policy. The MEMORY/house style clearly values this (OS-Project has it).

P2 — code-review.md: author prep, reviewer checklist that cites the other standards, PR size, the grep-every-rename post-fix sweep discipline (a recurrence the user already codified in OS-Project), solo-phase self-review. Pairs with security-review gate.

P2 — logging-and-observability.md: structured logging, levels, MANDATORY secret/API-key redaction (ties to principle §6), run-event vs log distinction, how the SSE/run-event stream relates to logs, what desktop/CLI/VS Code surfaces emit. OS-Project's exists; retarget from no_std ring buffers to a Node/Tauri context.

P3 (consider) — infrastructure.md / release.md equivalents: toolchain pinning, dependency-addition policy (directly relevant: the multi-LLM 'adopt a thin TS library only if a named trigger fires' stance belongs in a dependency policy), supply-chain, and a per-surface release standard (the release-a-surface runbook exists but a binding release standard does not). localization.md is likely N/A given the English-only + Phase-1 scope, but a one-line 'English-only docs/code' rule could fold into documentation-style or code-style.

CONSISTENCY — three reconciliations needed regardless of new files: (1) strike the Vercel AI SDK from architectural-principles §2 and documentation-style §5 and replace with the @relavium/llm internal-abstraction language; this must land in the same change as the ADR that supersedes ADR-0004. (2) Resolve the Proposed-vs-Accepted ADR-status contradiction across adr-template.md, decisions/README.md, and documentation-style §7. (3) When ADR-0004 is superseded, the new principle wording in architectural-principles §2 should cite the superseding ADR, per the project's own 'a proposal that breaks a principle needs an ADR' rule.

HOUSE-STYLE ALIGNMENT — the user's MEMORY notes OS-Project as the preferred reference for the docs taxonomy + ADR format. Relavium's standards README already mirrors the OS-Project 'standard vs ADR vs guide' framing well; completing the standards set to match OS-Project's coverage (code/test/review/commit/security/error/logging) is the concrete way to honor the documented house standard.

newFiles.purpose: The new ADR (next free number 0011) carrying the full multi-LLM decision and rationale: build an internal Relavium-owned @relavium/llm abstraction exposing a single provider-agnostic LLMProvider seam (modelId + messages + tools in -> normalized chunk stream + cost record out; no vendor type ever crosses it), implemented day-one by thin hand-rolled adapters over each provider's official TS SDK (Anthropic + Gemini dedicated, OpenAI + DeepSeek sharing one OpenAI-compatible adapter). Records the internal-first migration stance with named triggers (sustained provider-drift cost; a must-have capability not economically hand-rollable) and the explicit 'never the Vercel AI SDK' rule. Follows adr-template.md; Related to 0003, 0006, 0008, the renamed 0004, and tech-stack.md. The seam itself is the immovable contract; the adapter implementation is deliberately reversible.

Canonical reference home for the immovable LLMProvider seam and its normalized types: the request shape (modelId + messages + tools), the normalized streamed chunk union, the usage/cost record (integer micro-cents, consistent with database-schema.md), and the rule that no vendor type crosses the seam. This is the DRY home the reference quadrant currently lacks; agent-yaml-spec.md and shared-core/README.md repoint here, and architecture/multi-llm-providers.md + ADR-0011 cite it.

Binding TypeScript source-code standard for the monorepo (the highest-priority missing standard): module/import rules, the no-vendor-type-across-the-seam discipline, Zod-schema-as-source-of-truth, error-shape conventions, naming. architectural-principles.md repeatedly promises code rules ('every surface', 'clean execution interface') but no binding source-code standard exists; the user's own house standard (OS-Project) ships 14 standards vs Relavium's 4.

Binding testing standard (Vitest unit + Playwright e2e): the engine-first build order in architectural-principles.md §1 assumes 'full unit test coverage' of packages/core/llm/shared before any surface exists, but no standard codifies what that means. Second-highest-priority gap mirroring the OS-Project standards set.
